pages.text: Review Super absorbing polymers as an internal curing agent for mitigation of early-age cracking of high-performance concrete bridge decks Bart Craeye a,b,*, Matthew Geirnaert a, Geert De Schutter a a Magnel Laboratory for Concrete Research, Ghent University, Technologiepark-Zwijnaarde 904, 9052 Ghent, Belgium b Artesis University College of Antwerp, Applied Engineering & Technology, Paardenmarkt 92, 2000 Antwerp, Belgium article info Article history: Received 18 November 2009 Received in revised form 30 April 2010 Accepted 19 June 2010 Available online 10 July 2010 Keywords: High-performance concrete Internal curing Super absorbing polymers Autogenous shrinkage Early-age cracking abstract High-performance concrete (HPC) with low w/b-ratio experiences a considerable chemical shrinkage and self-desiccation during its hydration process, leading to a rather high autogenous shrinkage deformation during hardening. In case the free deformation of the concrete is prevented, internal stresses are intro- duced, which can lead to premature cracks. These early-age cracks can severely affect the durability of a concrete structure. By adding super absorbing polymers (SAP) into the HPC as an internal curing agent, and by adding additional curing water to the concrete mixture, the chemical shrinkage and the self-des- iccation during hydration of the concrete is counteracted and thus the autogenous shrinkage of the HPC can be signiﬁcantly reduced. Unfortunately, this process of internal curing also has some disadvantageous effects on the mechanical properties. In search of an optimization of the internal curing process, an exten- sive experimental program was performed on HPC, using different degrees of internal curing, to assess the mechanical and thermal properties of the HPC, and to evaluate the effectiveness of the performed cur- ing. The goal is to obtain a maximal autogenous shrinkage reduction and a minimal strength reduction. The resulting effect on the early-age cracking risk is simulated by means of ﬁnite element calculations. The simulations also include thermal stress development due to the heat of hydration. In case 70 kg/ m 3 of internal curing water is provided via the SAP, an optimal reduction of the cracking risk is noticed, mainly caused by the autogenous shrinkage reduction and the appearing expansive deformation peak directly after setting takes place. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. the membrane, used for volumetric measurements, is not entirely eliminated [33]. At the Magnel Laboratory for Concrete Research, Ghent Univer- sity, a vertical dilatometer for concrete has been developed ( Fig. 1) [3]. The fresh concrete is cast into a vertical cylindrical Teﬂon tube and compacted on a vibrating table. The length of the tube is 30 cm and the tube has an internal diameter of 6 cm. Additional Teﬂon foil is provided to reduce friction between the concrete and the mold. Linear vertical shrinkage measurements can start immedi- ately after casting, by means of LVDT-transducers placed on top of a Teﬂon lid, that follows the shrinkage behaviour of the concrete by means of a bolt. Shrinkage measurements are performed on two identical specimens in parallel. At the same time temperature mea- surements are performed on a third specimen, in order to take into account the effect of the heat of hydration on the autogenous shrinkage results. The tests are performed inside a climate room (20 /C176C, 60% RH). Evaporation is eliminated by using plastic foil around the concrete and by using Teﬂon with a low absorbing coef- ﬁcient as the formwork material. 3. Internal curing by means of SAP 3.1. External curing versus internal curing of HPC High-performance concrete with low w/b-ratio develops a low permeability. Therefore, it can be assumed that conventional exter- nal curing techniques may be relatively non effective in preventing self-desiccation in the centre of a thick concrete layer. In that case, internal curing needs to be performed, which is a very promising technique that provides additional moisture in the concrete for more effective hydration of the cement and reduces self-desicca- tion [13]. A curing agent is added into the concrete to provide an internal water reservoir that gradually releases its water during hydration. Currently, two applied internal curing techniques are used with diverse success rates. For optimum performance, the internal curing agent should possess high water absorption capac- ity and must have the ability to easily release the absorbed water under pressure. Two types of curing agents ﬁt these descriptions [4]: – Saturated lightweight aggregates (LWA). – Super absorbing polymers (SAP). Our focus lies on the use of SAP as an internal curing agent. An efﬁcient curing is obtained if all pores in the cement matrix remain ﬁlled with water and if all of the curing water is driven out of the curing agents. In addition to the reduction in autogenous shrink- age, other beneﬁts of adding SAP to a concrete can be named: – The ability to obtain a higher ultimate degree of hydration of the cement (due to the maintenance of a higher internal relative humidity), and thus a higher compressive strength at long term [16]. – The pores and air voids formed inside the matrix due to the SAP after drying can help in obtaining a frost resistance. Therefore, SAP act as an air entrainment agent and can protect the con- crete against freeze/thaw cycles [17]. In this study, the effect of SAP on the mechanical and thermal properties of HPC will be investigated. The main focus is the reduc- tion of autogenous shrinkage of HPC and the evaluation of the ad- justed cracking behaviour of concrete structures by adding SAP and additional curing water. Thermal effects due to the heat of hydra- tion will also be included in this study. 3.2. Previous studies SAP have a very high ability to absorb water, sometimes even up to 500 times their ow

ment to the reference mortar, successive reduction of autogenous shrinkage is obtained and also a higher internal relative humidity was found. An expansive swelling peak is noticed in the ﬁrst hours of hydration ( Fig. 2 ). If there is a continuous supply of water to a concrete during hydration, the concrete expands as a result of absorption of water by the cement gel. Addition of 0.6% SAP wt.% of cement to a mortar, reduces the compressive strength by 19% [2b]. Geiker et al. [16] added SAP (0.4 wt.% of cement, diameters up to 250 lm) to mortars with w/c-ratio of 0.35. A clear reduction of autogenous shrinkage was obtained and even 20% higher com- pressive strength was found after 28 days due to the higher ulti- mate degree of hydration. The effect of SAP on the early-age behaviour of HPC was studied by Piérard et al. [15]. They found a reduction of 50–65% in autog- enous shrinkage after 6 days by adding respectively 0.3–0.6% SAP wt.% of total powder mass to the reference HPC. The compressive strength after 28 days was reduced by respectively 14% and 8%. Craeye [3] also found that addition of SAP (diameters between 100 lm and 800 lm) is efﬁcient in reducing autogenous shrinkage of HPC (Fig. 3). Different amounts of one type of SAP were added to reach the aimed amount of internal curing water of 30 kg/m 3, 40 kg/m3 and 50 kg/m 3. This leads to a autogenous shrinkage reduction of respectively 51%, 58% and 68% after 144 h of harden- ing. The strength reduction after 28 days is respectively 16%, 27% and 31%. According to Igarashi and Watanabe [18], internal curing via SAP is more effective in case lower w/c-ratios are applied. 3.3. Required internal curing water The success of the internal curing is greatly depending on the amount of internal curing water added to the concrete and en- trapped in the SAP. Furthermore it is important that this internal curing water is released at the right moment and is available all over the hydrating cement matrix. The required amount of internally curing water can be esti- mated theoretically by using Powers’ model [4]: W C /C18/C19 IC ¼ 0:18 /C1 W C /C18/C19 for W C /C18/C19 6 0:36 ð1Þ For the reference concrete composition considered in this study (Table 1, w/c-ratio equals 0.32 and a total amount of 475 kg/m 3 of cement is added), the required internal water amount equals 27 kg/m3. These predictive formulas underestimate the required amount of internal curing water mainly due to the restricted mobility of the water inside the hardening cementitious system (‘travel distance’ about 200 lm according to [14]). As a conse- quence, and taking into consideration the results of a previous study [3], in the experimental outline of this study higher amounts of internal curing water will be added to the concrete mixture in order to reduce the autogenous shrinkage: 50 kg/m 3, 70 kg/m 3 and 90 kg/m 3. 4. Testing procedure 4.1. Concrete compositions Within the experimental research program concerning the mit- igation of autogenous shrinkage, a high performance reference Fig. 3. Mitigation of the autogenous deformation of HPC by adding super absorbing polymers [3]. Table 1 Concrete mix compositions (in kg/m 3). Craeye et al.

concrete (REF) was considered with the following composition, according to Table 1 . The w/c-ratio is 0.32. The w/b ratio is 0.30. The superplasticizer is a polycarboxylic ether. Porphyry coarse aggregates were chosen, having a particle size mainly varying between 2 mm and 20 mm. The silica fume, containing more than 90% of amorphous SiO 2, has a speciﬁc surface area of 20 m 2/g. This HPC, with a low w/b ra- tio, will be very vulnerable to early-age autogenous shrinkage, also due to the use of a rapid hardening cement and the addition of sil- ica fume [3,13]. A previous study [11], with a similar HPC compo- sition (w/b ratio of 0.33), indicates tensile failure of the HPC after 6 days due to internal restraint of the autogenous shrinkage. In or- der to mitigate the autogenous shrinkage and to prevent early-age cracking, additional internal curing water will be provided by means of SAP. The SAP used, is a suspension polymerized, cova- lently cross linked acrylamide/acrylic acid copolymler. The particle density is 785 kg/m 3 (diameter between 80 lm and 150 lm) and has a water absorption capacity of 45 g/g after 5 min (the approx- imate mixing time). Based on this latter absorption level, the amount of SAP to be added to the concrete is estimated, aiming for an amount of internal curing water equal to 50 kg/m 3 (SAP50), 70 kg/m 3 (SAP70) and 90 kg/m 3 (SAP90). This leads to a corresponding SAP amount of respectively 1.11 kg/m 3, 1.56 kg/m 3 and 2.00 kg/m 3. The amount of curing water itself has to be added to the concrete during mixing. As compensation, the sand content is reduced in order to obtain 1 m 3 of concrete ( Table 1 ). Concrete mixes of 0.2 m 3 are made using a planetary mixer according to the following mixing procedure: ﬁrst the dry components (binder, ﬁne and coarse aggregates, SAP) are mixed for 1 min, and after- wards the water and superplasticizer are added and mixing contin- ues for another 4 min [6]. An intensive experimental program is performed to study the effect of internal curing on different types of concrete properties [6]: (i) fresh properties (slump, ﬂow, density); (ii) mechanical properties (autogenous shrinkage, basic creep, modulus of elastic- ity, compressive strength and tensile strength); and (iii) thermal properties (hydration heat production). 4.2. Fresh concrete properties The fresh concrete properties and thus the consistency of the concrete mixture can be determined by means of the slump test and the ﬂow test according to the Belgian Code NBN EN 12350-2 and NBN EN 12350-5. Also the density of the fresh concrete can be easily determined by measuring the net weight of a reservoir with a known volume ﬁlled with compacted HPC. In order to ob- tain similar slump and ﬂow values, the amount of superplasticizer of the four fresh concrete mixes is adjusted. 4.3. Mechanical properties 4.3.1. Autogenous shrinkage and basic creep The test method described in paragraph 2.2 is being used to determine the early-age autogenous shrinkage immediately after mixing and during the ﬁrst 6 days. Three prismatic test pieces 150 mm /C2 150 mm cross section and 500 mm in height are used for the determination of the long term autogenous shrinkage and the basic creep behaviour according to the Belgian Code NBN B15-228. After mixing and vibrating, the test pieces are placed in a climate room (20 /C176C, 90% RH) for 24 h. After demolding, these specimens are covered with an adhesive aluminium foil to prevent drying and each surface

5.2.2. Basic creep The basic creep can be obtained by subtracting the instanta- neous deformation (when loading is placed on top of the test pieces or adjusted) and the autogenous shrinkage ( Fig. 5 ) from the mean value of the total deformation. Higher amounts of SAP and internal curing water lead to higher basic creep values (Fig. 6 ). After 63 days the basic creep is respectively /C0 480 lm/m, /C0 595 lm/m, /C0 626 lm/m and /C0 660 lm/m for REF, SAP50, SAP70 and SAP90. Generally, the creep in concrete with low w/b-ratio is smaller than in concrete with high w/b-ratio, ascribed to the lower water content and the formation of a more rigid structure in con- crete with a lower w/b-ratio [11]. 5.2.3. Compressive strength and tensile strength Internal curing with SAP leads towards a strength reduction (Fig. 7 and Table 3). Due to the additional amount of internal curing water, the w/b-ratio will increase and thus both the compressive strength and the tensile splitting strength will decrease. Another explanation can be found in the addition of SAP that can lead to a higher amount of larger pores (up to 200 lm) and a reduction of the amount of smaller pores, that are assumed to be ﬁlled with hydration products [30]. The SAP acts as an air entrainment agent [17]. These encapsulated air voids can have a disadvantageous ef- fect on the (compressive) strength of a concrete. It can be assumed that the compressive strength of a concrete decreases by 5% if 1% of air is added [23]. The reduction in tensile strength is quite similar (in %) to the reduction in compressive strength. 0 10 20 30 40 50 0 7 14 21 28 Time (days) E (GPa) REF SAP50 SAP70 SAP90 Fig. 8. ccr ¼ fct r ð2aÞ Rearranging Eq. (2a) gives the cracking criteria for real structures as shown in the following equation: r fct;lab 6 0:75 ð2bÞ If the tensile stress to tensile strength (determined in laboratory conditions) ratio is smaller than 0.75, no cracking will occur. Ten- sile stresses are taken positive, compressive stresses are taken negative. Fig. 11 clearly indicates that there are two regions that are par- ticularly vulnerable to high early-age tensile stresses: the top sur- face of the bridge deck experiences high horizontal tensile stresses Sxx, and the lower corner of the deck is a zone with high early-age vertical tensile stresses Szz. The shear stresses are inferior com- pared to the normal stresses. Looking at the development in time of Sxx at the critical zone in REF (point 1, Fig. 13), the Sxx/fct ratio ex- ceeds 0.75 after 42 h and thus cracking is expected. This early-age cracking nature, with lengthwise cracks (out of plane direction), actually is observed in practice [31]. The development of the Syy/fct ratio in REF comes close to 0.75 but does not exceed this value ( Fig. 14), so no cracking in horizon- tal direction is expected and observed [31] in point 3. The assumed cracking criteria (2b), with the critical value of 0.75, gives a good estimation of the cracking occurrence. Finally the effectiveness of the internal curing is evaluated. By adding an additional amount of internal curing water and SAP to REF, the maximum stresses in horizontal ( x) and vertical ( y) direc- tion decrease and the time of appearance is faster ( Table 5 ). Eval- uation of the cracking behaviour by means of the time dependent development of the Sxx/fct ratio and the Syy/fct ratio (the values of these ratio’s decrease), indicates a reduced cracking risk by adding SAP in the crack sensitive zones ( Figs. 13 and 14). These ratio’s are slightly higher for SAP90 and SAP50 in compari- son with SAP70, which indicates that the optimal amount of addi- tional internal curing water is approximately 70 kg/m 3 in order to effectively reduce the early-age cracking behaviour of the refer- ence HPC (REF). The appearing swelling peak, noticed during regis- tration of the early-age autogenous deformation ( Fig. 4 ), and the reduction of the stiffness due to addition of SAP ( Fig. 8 ) can be a possible explanation for the reduced cracking risk. 7. Conclusions High-performance concrete with low w/b-ration experiences high autogenous deformation at early age. By adding super absorb- ing polymers (SAP) into the HPC as an internal curing agent, and by adding additional curing water to the concrete mixture, the chem- ical shrinkage and the self-desiccation during hydration of the con- crete is counteracted and thus the autogenous shrinkage of the HPC can be signiﬁcantly reduced. Several thermal and mechanical tests and ﬁnite element calculations were performed to evaluate the effect of internal curing on the high-performance concrete properties and cracking behaviour. Therefore one reference com- position was compared to three compositions with additional amount of SAP, partially replacing the sand of the reference com- position. In addition, this study indicates the effectiveness of super absorbing polymers (SAP) as an internal curing agent to prevent early-age cracking of bridge decks of high-performance concrete. The main conclusions of this study are listed below: – By adding SAP, the autogenous shrinkage is reduced, mainly due to appearance of a swell

behaviour [19]. Therefore, the use of a reasonable approximate va- lue is sufﬁcient. The thermal conductivity is taken 1.61 W/(m K) for all mixes [22]. A heat capacity of 1000 J/(kg K) is reasonable for HPC [23]. The thermal strains are directly linked to the temperature evo- lution by the coefﬁcient of thermal dilation (CTD). This parameter tends rapidly to a constant value which only depends on the con- crete mix [24]. The thermal dilation coefﬁcient has an inﬂuence on the maximal strain value in a concrete structure [19]. A porphyry based concrete has a CTD of 10.0 /C2 10/C0 6//C176C [23]. The use of internal curing does not affect the value of the CTD [13]. 5. Results and discussion 5.1. Fresh properties In order to have a comparable consistency of the four mixtures, the amount of superplasticizer is adjusted. The more internal cur- ing water is added, the less superplasticizer that is needed in order to obtain similar slump and ﬂow values ( Table 2). The densities of the fresh concrete compositions decrease with increasing amount of curing water. This can be explained by the partial replacement of sand of the reference concrete by the internal curing water, in order to obtain 1 m 3 of concrete. For hardened concrete (age of 28 days), the decreased density with successively increased amount of SAP is also found ( Table 2 ). 5.2. Mechanical properties 5.2.1. Autogenous shrinkage The resulting early-age autogenous deformations measured by means of the vertical dilatometer are shown in Fig. 4. It is clear that addition of SAP leads towards a reduction in autogenous shrinkage. However, no complete elimination of autogenous shrinkage is obtained. The autogenous deformations are zeroed at a concrete age of 6 h (time zero). Before 6 h, some deviating results are obtained, most probably due to the effect of some bleeding water and due to the settlement in fresh concrete of the sensor (LVDT on Teﬂon lid) be- fore the time of setting. For real concrete structures, shrinkage deformations become important as soon as stresses can be built up, thus once time zero has passed. On the material level, this re- quires a percolating structure of hydration products, giving strength and stiffness to the cementitious material [25,26]. The de- gree of hydration corresponding to the onset of strength and stiff- ness development is called the percolation threshold a0. For the considered HPC ( Table 1), a0 is equal to 0.08, which on a time scale can be translated to a time-zero t0 equal to 6 h [3,6]. This explains why the autogenous deformation shrinkage curves are zeroed at the age of 6 h. After time zero has passed, a swelling peak occurs at approximately 11 h for the internally cured concretes. This phe- nomenon of early-age swelling is well known in literature and dif- ferent reasons can be mentioned to explain this expansive nature: (i) the reabsorption of bleeding water [1], (ii) the growth of further hydration reaction products inside the network that generates an internal pressure causing a moderate swelling of the system [28] and (iii) the ettringite formation [29]. In this study, this expansive peak is higher for the compositions with a higher amount of added SAP and internal curing water, possibly due to the premature deliberation of internal curing water at a moment self-desiccation is not important yet ( Fig. 4 ). In the reference composition, no swelling peak is noticed because no additional curing water is pro- vided. When the swelling peak is
